fragments.content: Peripheral neuropathy refers to damage of peripheral nerves, affecting ~2.4% of general population, up to 8% of elderly. Classification: by distribution (mononeuropathy, mononeuritis multiplex, polyneuropathy), by fiber type (sensory, motor, autonomic; large-fiber vs small-fiber), by pathology (axonal degeneration vs demyelination). Common causes: diabetes (most common, distal symmetric sensorimotor), alcohol, B12 deficiency, hypothyroidism, renal failure, medications (chemotherapy, amiodarone, metronidazole), autoimmune (CIDP, vasculitis), hereditary (Charcot-Marie-Tooth), infections (HIV, Lyme, leprosy), toxins. Small-fiber neuropathy affects pain/temperature fibers (burning pain, autonomic symptoms) with normal NCS; diagnosed by skin biopsy showing reduced intraepidermal nerve fiber density. Large-fiber neuropathy affects proprioception/vibration, causes sensory ataxia, areflexia, abnormal NCS.

Neuropathy treatment addresses underlying cause and symptomatic pain management. Cause-specific: glycemic control for diabetic neuropathy (prevents progression, limited reversal), B12 replacement, thyroid hormone, alcohol cessation, immunotherapy for autoimmune (IVIG, steroids, plasma exchange for CIDP; steroids/immunosuppression for vasculitis), discontinuation of offending drugs. Neuropathic pain management: first-line includes gabapentinoids (gabapentin 300-3600mg/day, pregabalin 150-600mg/day—dose adjust for renal function), SNRIs (duloxetine 60-120mg/day, venlafaxine), TCAs (amitriptyline/nortriptyline 25-150mg at bedtime—anticholinergic/cardiac side effects limit use in elderly). Second-line: topical agents (lidocaine 5% patch for focal pain, capsaicin 8% patch), tramadol (short-term, addiction risk). Third-line: strong opioids (avoid chronic use), botulinum toxin for focal neuropathic pain. Combination therapy often needed. Non-pharmacologic: TENS, acupuncture, physical therapy, foot care for diabetic neuropathy. Refractory cases: spinal cord stimulation, dorsal root ganglion stimulation.

Neuropathy diagnosis requires: (1) History and examination: symptom distribution (length-dependent 'stocking-glove' vs non-length-dependent), fiber types involved (sensory: numbness, tingling, pain; motor: weakness, atrophy; autonomic: orthostasis, GI/GU dysfunction, sweating abnormalities). Neurological exam: sensory modalities (pinprick, temperature for small-fiber; vibration, proprioception for large-fiber), strength, reflexes (ankle jerks lost early), gait (sensory ataxia—worse with eyes closed, positive Romberg). (2) Laboratory screening: CBC, CMP, HbA1c/fasting glucose, B12 with methylmalonic acid, TSH, SPEP/UPEP, ESR/CRP, ANA/ENA if autoimmune suspected. (3) Electrodiagnostics: Nerve conduction studies (NCS) distinguish axonal (reduced amplitude) vs demyelinating (slow velocity, prolonged latency, conduction block) patterns; EMG assesses motor unit changes. (4) Specialized tests: skin biopsy for small-fiber neuropathy (intraepidermal nerve fiber density), quantitative sensory testing, autonomic testing (QSART, tilt-table), lumbar puncture (elevated protein in CIDP), genetic testing (hereditary neuropathies), nerve biopsy (vasculitis, amyloid).
